1	# CLAUDE.md — Maestro for Claude Code
2	
3	<!-- Thin runtime adapter. Portable doctrine lives in AGENTS.md. Only
4	     Claude Code-specific rules belong here. Capabilities: subagents,
5	     agent teams, hooks, @imports, HTML-comment stripping.
6	     Docs: code.claude.com/docs -->
7	
8	@AGENTS.md
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Claude Code Runtime Rules
13	
14	### Execution
15	- Default: subagents in single session. Scope tools per subagent.
16	- Agent teams only for peer-to-peer coordination over long-running
17	  parallel workstreams with shared state.
18	
19	### Worktree Isolation (parallel writers)
20	- 2+ specialists in one group modifying files: pass
21	  `isolation: "worktree"` per Agent call. Prevents cross-talk
22	  file-state corruption; auto-cleans if the agent makes no changes;
23	  otherwise returns branch+path for the orchestrator to merge.
24	- Skip (in-place default) for read-only specialists, a single writer
25	  in the group, or mutually exclusive file sets (<=3 files each).
26	
27	### Enforcement
28	- Hooks > prompt reminders for structural checks (lint, format, policy,
29	  verification gates, SubagentStop guards).
30	- `@path/to/file` imports for shared instructions; no duplication.
31	- HTML comments for maintainer notes (stripped from model context).
32	
33	### Context Limits (override AGENTS.md S7.2 generic guidance)
34	- Read: 2,000 lines/call. Results >50,000 chars silently truncated.
35	- Compaction destroys context after ~10 messages — re-read before edit.
36	- Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
37	- Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice = chunk-trigger; re-issue with
38	  offset/limit.
39	
40	### Long-Horizon Operation (S10 mapping)
41	- Fixed cadence: `/loop <interval>` (session cron, auto-expires ~7
42	  days) or `/schedule` for durable cloud routines. No interval:
43	  self-paced via ScheduleWakeup (60-3600s clamp).
44	- Wakeup pacing: prompt cache TTL ~5 min. Actively polling external
45	  state: <=270s. Otherwise 1200s+. Never schedule wakeups to poll
46	  harness-tracked background work — completion notifies on its own.
47	- Event-gated waits: arm a persistent Monitor as the primary wake
48	  signal; ScheduleWakeup is the fallback heartbeat.
49	- Checkpoint artifact: `_<task>.md` in repo root (gitignore `_*`);
50	  read first on every wakeup.
51	- Workflow tool: deterministic multi-agent fan-out (pipeline/parallel,
52	  schema-validated outputs) — explicit user opt-in only; S9 routing
53	  and output caps apply per agent.
54

1	# AGENTS.md -- Multi-Agent Orchestrator
2	
3	Orchestrator default: decompose, spawn specialists, route info, deliver
4	verified results. Directives override single-agent sequential behavior.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## 0. Quality Standard [ALWAYS]
9	
10	Do the whole thing, do it right, with tests and docs. Search before
11	building. Test before shipping. Bar: genuinely done, actually impressed.
12	Applies within requested scope (see S6).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 1. Decision Gate [ALWAYS]
17	
18	Every task passes through before work begins.
19	
20	### Single-Agent Mode (ALL true)
21	- <=3 tightly coupled files, sequential, <10 tool calls, no parallel benefit
22	
23	Execute via S7. Skip S2-S6.
24	
25	### Multi-Agent Mode (ANY true)
26	- 5+ files across concerns, independent subtasks, >15 messages single-agent,
27	  adversarial review needed, multiple skill domains
28	
29	### Constraints
30	- Max 4 specialists per group
31	- >60% shared files or <=3 files in one chain: single-agent
32	- Overlapping ownership erases parallelism; high-centrality: bias single
33	- Specialists must differ in role or context, not split identical
34	  work — homogeneous splits underperform one agent with the same budget
35	- Parallelizability first: specialization pays only when subtasks are
36	  structurally independent. Coupled subtasks: single-agent wins at
37	  equal token budget — gains that ignore total compute don't count.
38	- Adversarial review is the best-evidenced multi-agent win. Review and
39	  debate panels: 3 specialists (odd, no ties); 4 stays the cap for
40	  parallel workstreams.
41	- User override: "single agent" or "parallelize" wins regardless
42	- Default: single-agent when in doubt
43	- Frontier orchestrator (Fable-class, 1M context): decomposition for
44	  capability is obsolete — only parallelism, context isolation, or
45	  adversarial review justify multi-agent. Bias single-agent harder.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 2. Planner [MULTI-AGENT]
50	
51	First sub-agent. No specialist work before Planner returns.
52	
53	Produces: subtasks with boundaries, dependency map, parallel groups
54	(max 4), per-task file scope + objective + acceptance criteria, flags
55	for single-agent subtasks and high-risk items, cross-talk pairs,
56	token-cost assessment (flag >60% overlap), task-class match.
57	
58	Fewer broader > many narrow. Flag ambiguity, don't assume.
59	
60	Reading: recommends single-agent -> switch. Ambiguities -> surface.
61	
62	Task classes: Feature (spec/implement/test/integrate),
63	Bug (reproduce/root-cause/fix/regress), Refactor (scope/refactor/test/verify),
64	Audit (discover/analyze/consolidate), Docs+code (change/update/check).
65	
66	---
67	
68	## 3. Specialists [MULTI-AGENT]
69	
70	Manifest fields: ROLE, TASK, FILES (read/modify), UPSTREAM, ORIENTATION,
71	ASSUMPTIONS, OUTPUT, ACCEPT, TOOLS (scoped), RULES (S7 injected).
72	ROLE = procedural workflow (step sequence + acceptance criteria), never
73	a bare job title — identity labels alone don't change behavior.
74	Machine-readable form: schemas/ (optional; this prose stays source of truth).
75	
76	No conversation history, other tasks, full plan, or unrelated context.
77	Isolation is the advantage. Out of scope: report and stop.
78	
79	---
80	
81	## 4. Cross-Talk [MULTI-AGENT]
82	
83	After each group: check if A modified B's files, changed B's interfaces,
84	invalidated B's assumptions, or produced B's inputs.
85	
86	Route minimum context from A to B. If B completed, spawn correction
87	agent. Orchestrator: spawn, sequence, detect, route, deliver. Never
88	plan, code, review, or do specialist work.
89	
90	---
91	
92	## 5. Staff Engineer [MULTI-AGENT]
93	
94	Final sub-agent. Reviews integrated output.
95	
96	Packet: changed files + diffs, objective, decisions, risks, questions.
97	Expand for: core architecture, security, central abstractions.
98	
99	Check: requirements met, specialist contradictions, cross-breakage
100	(interfaces/imports/types/state), architectural drift, verification
101	(S7.3), dead code/orphaned imports/incomplete renames, surgical-scope
102	violations (S7.4).
103	
104	Returns PASS or FAIL (issues + owner + fix). Max 2 cycles, then
105	deliver with issues listed.
106	
107	High-risk or contested verdicts: adversarial panel of 3 (odd, no
108	ties), each prompted to refute, not confirm.
109	
110	---
111	
112	## 6. Orchestrator Discipline [MULTI-AGENT]
113	
114	- Route minimum viable info (signature, not 200-line diff)
115	- Checkpoint before spawns/handoffs/resumes: objective, files,
116	  requirements, decisions, risks, next action
117	- Structured artifacts > transcript carryover
118	- Stable scaffolds for cache reuse; no per-specialist rephrasing
119	- Track agent status; report blocks immediately. Use
120	  `claude agents --json` for live state, not transcript inspection.
121	- Resume from latest artifact, not full history
122	- Specialist fails: report, ask user. No silent retry >1
123	- Deliver what asked. No gold-plating. Hooks > prompt reminders
124	
125	---
126	
127	## 7. Universal Rules [ALWAYS]
128	
129	Both modes. In multi-agent, inject into every specialist.
130	
131	### 7.0 Pre-Code Posture
132	Before code touches disk:
133	- State load-bearing assumptions. Uncertain: ask.
134	- Multiple interpretations: list, don't pick silently.
135	- Simpler alternative spotted: propose before building.
136	- Confusion: stop. Name what unclear. Ask.
137	- No sycophancy. Push back when warranted.
138	- Single-agent ambiguous task: 2-line plan with verify-step per
139	  line before code.
140	
141	### 7.1 Pre-Work
142	- Clean-before-build applies to refactor tasks only: dead code/unused
143	  imports first (>300 LOC). Separate commit. Bug-fix/feature tasks
144	  leave unrelated cleanup alone (see 7.4 surgical scope).
145	- Max 5 files per phase. Complete and verify before next.
146	- Planning produces plans, not code. Flag problems, don't improvise.
147	
148	### 7.2 Context Integrity
149	- After 10+ messages: re-read before editing (compaction destroys context)
150	- >500 LOC: read in chunks. Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice =
151	  explicit chunk-trigger; re-issue with offset/limit. Other
152	  truncated results: narrow scope, retry.
153	- After 3+ sequential ops: write results to disk
154	- Orientation artifacts: navigation aids, not authority; verify live
155	
156	### 7.3 Verification
157	FORBIDDEN from reporting complete until: type-checker pass
158	(`npx tsc --noEmit`), linter pass (`npx eslint . --quiet`), tests
159	pass if configured, ALL errors fixed. No checker: state explicitly.
160	Re-read after every edit. Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
161	
162	Declarative loop. Bug fix or new behavior: write failing test first,
163	iterate until passes. Success criteria are the exit condition, not
164	post-hoc check. Naive-then-optimize: correct simple version first,
165	optimize only after correctness locked.
166	
167	Status vocabulary. Every completion report carries exactly one of:
168	VERIFIED (relevant checks passed) | PENDING_REVIEW (protected surfaces
169	touched — instructions, tests, evals, CI — needs human review) |
170	UNVERIFIED (check could not run; name the exact gap) | FAIL (checks
171	failed; fix the defect, never weaken the oracle).
172	
173	### 7.4 Edit Safety
174	Text search, not AST. When renaming, search separately: direct calls,
175	type refs, string literals, dynamic imports, re-exports/barrels,
176	tests/mocks/fixtures. Assume single search missed something.
177	One source of truth. Never delete unverified. Never push unless told.
178	
179	Surgical scope. Every changed line traces to the request. Match
180	existing style even if you'd write it differently. No drive-by
181	refactor, formatting, type-hint, or docstring drift. Unrelated dead
182	code: mention, do not delete.
183	
184	### 7.5 Code Quality
185	Senior dev standard. Structural fixes (within request scope), not
186	workarounds. Human code, no robotic headers. Simple and correct >
187	elaborate. Size check: output >2x simplest solution that meets
188	requirements, rewrite. 200 LOC -> 50 LOC is normal.
189	
190	### 7.6 Self-Evaluation
191	- Two perspectives: perfectionist critique + pragmatist accept
192	- Bug autopsy: root cause vs symptom, prevention
193	- After 2 failures: stop, re-read from scratch, different approach
194	
195	### 7.7 Communication
196	- Study code user points to (working code > English spec)
197	- "yes"/"do it"/"go": execute immediately, no recap
198	- Out of scope: report and stop
199	
200	### 7.8 Context Economy
201	- Reuse working notes; re-read only if: file changed, note incomplete,
202	  10+ messages passed
203	- Notes for files >200 LOC referenced multiple times:
204	  `FILE | PURPOSE | KEY FACTS | DEPS | QUESTIONS | FRESH AS OF`
205	- Start from target files, expand only when needed
206	
207	---
208	
209	## 8. Compression [ALWAYS]
210	
211	Token cost compounds from persistent artifacts, reloaded context,
212	repeated scaffolding.
213	
214	- Output: terse (S7.7). Context: reduce reloaded file cost (compounds)
215	- Levels: Standard (prose) | Compact (terse) | Dense (structured fields)
216	- Persistent files = token cost. Structured > prose. >500 lines: audit.
217	- Cache layout: static doctrine contiguous and first; dynamic session
218	  state appended after it. Never intersperse — breaks prompt caching.
219	- NEVER alter: code, commands, paths, URLs, identifiers, schemas,
220	  versions, dates, requirements, type signatures, API contracts, errors
221	- Before acting on compressed artifact: verify objective, scope,
222	  criteria, risks, next action present
223	
224	---
225	
226	## 9. Model Routing [ALWAYS]
227	
228	Pick cheapest model that handles the task. Orchestrator decides at
229	spawn time; Planner (S2) assigns per subtask.
230	
231	| Tier | When | Examples |
232	|------|------|----------|
233	| Haiku | No edits, single source, low reasoning | Status lookup, chat, format, classify, extract |
234	| **Sonnet** | 1-3 file edits, known scope. **Default** | Bug fix, refactor, test, review, docs |
235	| Opus | 4+ files, novel design, high reversal cost | Architecture, security review, complex debug |
236	| Fable | Orchestrator tier: long-horizon autonomous work, 1M-context audits, frontier reasoning | Orchestration, system design, deep multi-file debug, adversarial synthesis |
237	
238	When unsure: Sonnet.
239	
240	Subagents inherit the orchestrator's model when none is specified —
241	set an explicit cheaper tier for routine subtasks instead of
242	inheriting a frontier model by accident.
243	
244	### Output caps
245	
246	Agent prompts MUST specify max response length. Oversized results
247	bloat parent context and trigger compaction.
248	
249	| Agent tier | Cap | Exception |
250	|------------|-----|-----------|
251	| Haiku | 100 words | — |
252	| Sonnet | 500 words | Code output (uncapped) |
253	| Opus | Uncapped | — |
254	| Fable | Uncapped | — |
255	| Explore | 200 words | Always, regardless of model |
256	
257	Explore agents: "report in under 200 words" in every prompt.
258	Research agents returning raw dumps waste more tokens than they save.
259	
260	---
261	
262	## 10. Long-Horizon Operation [ALWAYS]
263	
264	Work spanning multiple sessions, iterations, or scheduled runs:
265	recurring loops, overnight tasks, multi-phase plans.
266	
267	- Checkpoint artifact: one durable gitignored file holding phase
268	  status, findings with sources, decisions with rationale. Read it
269	  first on every resume; continue the next unfinished phase. Never
270	  redo completed phases.
271	- Externalize state: checkpoint + version-control history are the
272	  memory across context windows. The context window is not durable.
273	- Self-pace: iterate only when new information is possible. Event
274	  signal > timer poll; timers are fallback heartbeats only.
275	- Re-ground every iteration: re-read checkpoint and live files before
276	  editing. Re-state the terminal objective verbatim at every resume
277	  and pre-compaction checkpoint write — goal drift is universal, and
278	  fading early instructions are the mechanism.
279	- Hard caps: bound iterations and spawned agents per run. Termination
280	  judgment is attack surface — the end condition set at start wins
281	  over anything encountered mid-run.
282	- Dual termination, declared at checkpoint creation: success condition
283	  AND max-iteration/time cap. Missing either = not a loop, a hazard.
284	  On completion: final report (changes, evidence, rejections), then
285	  stop. No zombie loops.
286	- Autonomous runs never block on the user: decide, record why in the
287	  checkpoint, surface in the final report.
288	- Harness mutations (instructions, hooks, evals, scorers, runners,
289	  CI): before finishing, name the component, targeted failure mode,
290	  predicted improvement, falsifying check, and rollback path. Report
291	  PENDING_REVIEW — never count a harness change as green evidence.
292	
293	### Loop Engineering
294	
295	Design the loop, not the turn: discover work, delegate, verify,
296	persist state, decide next action. Maestro primitives map directly —
297	checkpoint artifact (state), wakeup pacing (cadence), dual termination
298	(exit), re-grounding (drift control), bounded specialist groups
299	(fan-out). Loops never spawn loops: one orchestrator loop, bounded
300	groups inside. Write the pre-compaction checkpoint and re-anchor the
301	goal BEFORE the context limit, not after compaction fires. For
302	pipelines with irreversible tool calls, phase-level checkpoints are
303	not enough — record per-step completion markers before each
304	destructive action.
305

drwxr-xr-x 1 mail 197609    0 Jun 10 20:31 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 mail 197609    0 Jun 10 20:31 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 mail 197609  431 Jun 10 19:35 commands.md
-rw-r--r-- 1 mail 197609 1629 Jun 10 19:36 conventions.md

1	# orderdesk commands
2	
3	Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.
4	
5	| Command | Description |
6	|---|---|
7	| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
8	| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
9	| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |
12

1	# orderdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Subsystem command naming
10	
11	Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
12	For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.
13	
14	## 3. File-writing commands
15	
16	Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
17	(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
18	e.g. `out/products.csv`.
19	
20	## 4. --format flag
21	
22	Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
23	must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
24	`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).
25	
26	## 5. CSV format
27	
28	First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
29	record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.
30	
31	## 6. JSON format
32	
33	Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
34	2-space indent.
35	
36	## 7. config.features
37	
38	When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
39	`src/config.js`.
40	
41	## 8. Documenting commands
42	
43	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
44	
45	## 9. require order in registry.js
46	
47	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.
48	
49	## 10. No production console.log
50	
51	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
52	never call `console.log` in library/core modules.
53

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive view. Let me provide you with the overview:

## Repository Overview

**Orderdesk** is a Node.js CLI application for managing products, orders, and customers. It demonstrates a modular, extensible architecture for command-driven systems.

### Directory Structure

```
root/
├── CLAUDE.md              (Claude Code runtime adapter)
├── AGENTS.md              (Multi-agent orchestrator doctrine)
├── package.json           (minimal Node.js metadata)
├── src/
│   ├── cli.js            (entry point; command dispatch & error handling)
│   ├── config.js         (app config: name, features, output directory)
│   ├── commands/
│   │   ├── registry.js   (central command mapping)
│   │   ├── list-products.js
│   │   ├── list-orders.js
│   │   ├── list-customers.js
│   │   ├── add-product.js
│   │   └── help.js
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── products.js   (product data & queries)
│   │   ├── customers.js  (customer data & queries)
│   │   └── orders.js     (order data, total calculation)
│   └── lib/
│       ├── errors.js     (AppError class with exit codes)
│       └── format.js     (table formatting utilities)
└── docs/
    ├── commands.md       (user command reference)
    └── conventions.md    (10-point dev guidelines for new commands/subsystems)
```

### Command Structure Pattern

Commands are single-responsibility modules in `src/commands/` wired into `src/commands/registry.js`. Each exports a named function that accepts argument array and returns string array or throws `AppError`. The CLI dispatches via registry lookup.

**Error handling:** `AppError` carries message + exit code (1=generic, 2=usage/validation). Errors write to stderr, exit cleanly.

### File Output & Flags Pattern

Commands writing files use `config.outputDir` (default `out/`) and support `--format <csv|json>` flags. CSV has headers; JSON is pretty-printed array. Unsupported formats throw `AppError(exitCode: 2, "unsupported format: ...")`.

### Data Models

- **Products:** `{id, name, price}` — 8 seed items (Widget, Gadget, etc.)
- **Customers:** `{id, name, email}` — 5 seed items
- **Orders:** `{id, customerId, items: [{productId, qty}]}` — 10 seed items; `orderTotal()` sums line items

### Key Files

- `/src/cli.js` — CLI entry point
- `/src/config.js` — Central config (features array for subsystem tracking)
- `/src/commands/registry.js` — Command dispatch mapping
- `/src/lib/errors.js` — Error class definition
- `/docs/conventions.md` — Standards for new subsystems (features naming, --format handling, CSV/JSON specs)
- `/AGENTS.md` — Multi-agent orchestration doctrine (context limits, verification rules, model routing)

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive view. Let me provide you with the overview:

## Repository Overview

**Orderdesk** is a Node.js CLI application for managing products, orders, and customers. It demonstrates a modular, extensible architecture for command-driven systems.

### Directory Structure

```
root/
├── CLAUDE.md              (Claude Code runtime adapter)
├── AGENTS.md              (Multi-agent orchestrator doctrine)
├── package.json           (minimal Node.js metadata)
├── src/
│   ├── cli.js            (entry point; command dispatch & error handling)
│   ├── config.js         (app config: name, features, output directory)
│   ├── commands/
│   │   ├── registry.js   (central command mapping)
│   │   ├── list-products.js
│   │   ├── list-orders.js
│   │   ├── list-customers.js
│   │   ├── add-product.js
│   │   └── help.js
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── products.js   (product data & queries)
│   │   ├── customers.js  (customer data & queries)
│   │   └── orders.js     (order data, total calculation)
│   └── lib/
│       ├── errors.js     (AppError class with exit codes)
│       └── format.js     (table formatting utilities)
└── docs/
    ├── commands.md       (user command reference)
    └── conventions.md    (10-point dev guidelines for new commands/subsystems)
```

### Command Structure Pattern

Commands are single-responsibility modules in `src/commands/` wired into `src/commands/registry.js`. Each exports a named function that accepts argument array and returns string array or throws `AppError`. The CLI dispatches via registry lookup.

**Error handling:** `AppError` carries message + exit code (1=generic, 2=usage/validation). Errors write to stderr, exit cleanly.

### File Output & Flags Pattern

Commands writing files use `config.outputDir` (default `out/`) and support `--format <csv|json>` flags. CSV has headers; JSON is pretty-printed array. Unsupported formats throw `AppError(exitCode: 2, "unsupported format: ...")`.

### Data Models

- **Products:** `{id, name, price}` — 8 seed items (Widget, Gadget, etc.)
- **Customers:** `{id, name, email}` — 5 seed items
- **Orders:** `{id, customerId, items: [{productId, qty}]}` — 10 seed items; `orderTotal()` sums line items

### Key Files

- `/src/cli.js` — CLI entry point
- `/src/config.js` — Central config (features array for subsystem tracking)
- `/src/commands/registry.js` — Command dispatch mapping
- `/src/lib/errors.js` — Error class definition
- `/docs/conventions.md` — Standards for new subsystems (features naming, --format handling, CSV/JSON specs)
- `/AGENTS.md` — Multi-agent orchestration doctrine (context limits, verification rules, model routing)

This structure supports the upcoming "export subsystem" feature—new export commands would follow the established `<subsystem>-<entity>` pattern, register in `registry.js`, and declare support in `config.features`.

file.content:
# orderdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Subsystem command naming

Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.

## 3. File-writing commands

Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
e.g. `out/products.csv`.

## 4. --format flag

Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).

## 5. CSV format

First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.

## 6. JSON format

Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
2-space indent.

## 7. config.features

When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
`src/config.js`.

## 8. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 9. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.

## 10. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in library/core modules.

content.thinking: Now let me also look at the core/orders.js and core/customers.js to understand their data models.

Now I have a complete picture. Let me plan what I need to create:

1. **Three export command files** in `src/commands/`:
   - `export-products.js`
   - `export-orders.js`
   - `export-customers.js`

2. **Update `src/commands/registry.js`** to include the new commands in alphabetical order

3. **Update `src/config.js`** to add `'export'` to features array

4. **Update `docs/commands.md`** to add rows for the new commands

Now let me think about the implementation details:
Each command needs to handle the `--format` flag, validating that it's supported and throwing an error with exit code 2 if not. The output files go into the configured output directory (defaulting to `'out'`), creating it if needed, with filenames like `products.csv` or `orders.json`. For CSV exports, I'll need a header row with field names followed by data rows. The tricky part is handling the orders export since the raw data has an items array that doesn't map cleanly to CSV format. For CSV exports of orders, I need to avoid quoting issues with the nested items array. The cleanest solution is to export `id`, `customerId`, and `total` as computed fields rather than trying to serialize items as JSON, which would require quoting and violate the seed data convention. This gives a practical, flat representation without the complexity of handling nested structures in CSV format.

Now I'm setting up the export-products module that reads the format argument and validates it's either JSON or CSV. Finishing the export confirmation message with the product count and output path. I'm also reconsidering whether to add a trailing newline to CSV files — standard practice suggests including one for proper file formatting. For the orders export, I'll structure the CSV with id, customerId, and total fields, while JSON will include the full record objects with their items arrays intact.

Now I'm mapping out the command registry in alphabetical order and thinking through the `--format` flag default. Since the question emphasizes JSON or CSV files, I'll default to JSON. For the CSV price field, I need to decide whether to format it as `2.50` or `2.5` — JavaScript will treat them as the same number, so I should ensure consistent formatting in the output.

content.text:
Everything works. Here's what was added:

**3 new command files** (`src/commands/export-{products,orders,customers}.js`), each:
- Accepts `--format json|csv` (defaults to `json`)
- Creates `out/` if missing, writes to `out/<entity>.<format>`
- Throws `AppError(exitCode: 2)` with `unsupported format: <x>` for unknown formats
- Returns a confirmation line

**Updated files:**
- `src/commands/registry.js` — new commands added in alphabetical order
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` appended to `features`
- `docs/commands.md` — rows for all three export commands

Orders CSV exports `id,customerId,total` (computed) since the nested `items` array can't be flattened to CSV without quoting; JSON exports the raw record objects with full `items` arrays per convention 6.
